Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a widespread fungal infection that shows up as dark, scabby patches on leaves and fruit. It tends to be at its worst in spring and early summer, when cool, wet weather gives the fungus plenty of opportunity to spread.
symptoms: Oily, brownish-olive spots appearing on the upper surfaces of leaves
Spots gradually harden into dark brown or black scab-like lesions
Infected fruit develops rough, corky patches on its surface
Heavily infected leaves may turn yellow and fall off early

chemical: Start applying captan-based fungicides at green tip stage, then repeat every 7–10 days whenever the weather stays wet
Use myclobutanil (Rally) or trifloxystrobin (Flint) as a curative option - apply within 72 hours of an infection event for best results
Mancozeb sprays offer solid protective coverage; apply before expected rain to stay ahead of the fungus
cultural: Collect and dispose of fallen leaves in autumn - this removes the overwintering spores that fuel next year's outbreak
Prune trees regularly to open up the canopy, which helps air move through and keeps foliage drier
Switch to drip irrigation if possible - keeping water off the leaves goes a long way toward preventing infection
Consider planting scab-resistant varieties like Liberty, Freedom, or Enterprise for a long-term solution
organic: Apply sulfur-based sprays every 7to14 days starting at bud break as a season-long protective strategy
Copper-based fungicides (such as copper hydroxide) work well as a protectant before infection periods
Neem oil applications early in the season can help reduce the initial spread of the disease

Apple___Apple_scab.prevention: Keep an eye on weather forecasts for infection-friendly conditions - wet weather combined with temperatures between 6–24°C is the key risk window. Tools like RIMpro or NEWA can help you time your spray applications more accurately.

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is a fungal disease that attacks apple fruit, leaves, and bark. If left unchecked, it can cause serious fruit losses and lead to disfiguring cankers on branches.
symptoms: Circular purple spots with a tan center on leaves - often called frogeye leaf spot
Fruit begins rotting from the blossom end, gradually turning black and shriveling into mummified remains
Sunken, reddish-brown cankers with cracked bark appearing on branches
Tiny black fruiting bodies (pycnidia) visible on infected plant tissue under close inspection

chemical: Apply captan or thiophanate-methyl fungicides from the pink stage of bud development through to harvest
Strobilurin fungicides such as azoxystrobin or trifloxystrobin provide reliable, effective control
Ziram sprays help protect fruit from infection as it develops throughout the growing season
cultural: Prune out all dead or cankered wood during dry weather to stop the fungus from spreading
Remove any mummified fruit you find on trees or on the orchard floor - these are major sources of next year's infection
Be careful when pruning to avoid unnecessary wounds, and disinfect tools between cuts to prevent spreading the pathogen
Opening up the canopy through thoughtful pruning helps reduce the humid conditions the fungus loves
organic: Apply copper-based sprays in early spring, before bud break, to knock back the fungal population before growth begins
Follow up with sulfur sprays at 10-day intervals throughout the growing season
Removing all infected mummies and fallen debris from the orchard is the most important organic management step

Apple___Black_rot.prevention: Good orchard hygiene is your best defense. Prune out cankers every year, keep the orchard floor clean, and try to avoid physical damage to fruit and bark that could invite infection.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: Cedar apple rust has an unusual lifecycle - it needs two different host plants (Eastern red cedar and apple) to complete its life cycle. The result on apples is distinctive yellow-orange spotting on leaves and fruit.
symptoms: Bright yellow-orange spots appearing on the upper surfaces of leaves in spring
Tube-like orange structures (aecia) developing on the undersides of infected leaves
Fruit developing distorted, orange-spotted lesions
Heavily infected leaves dropping well before the end of the season

chemical: Apply myclobutanil or propiconazole from the pink bud stage through 3 - 4 weeks after bloom - this is the window that matters most
Trifloxystrobin (Flint) or azoxystrobin offer good systemic control that moves within the plant
Captan alone won't cut it against rust - it needs to be combined with a DMI fungicide to be effective
cultural: If practical, removing nearby Eastern red cedar or juniper trees breaks the disease cycle at its source
Planting rust-resistant apple varieties such as Liberty, Redfree, or Pristine eliminates most of the risk
In early spring, check nearby cedar trees and remove any visible orange galls before they have a chance to release spores
organic: Sulfur sprays applied from pink bud through cover offer some level of protection during the risk period
Copper fungicides applied before bloom may help reduce infection rates
Neem oil can help suppress early-season infections before they establish

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.prevention: The most vulnerable window is from pink bud through 2to3 weeks after petals fall. Focus your spray program tightly around this period for the best results.

Apple___healthy.description: This apple plant looks healthy - no signs of disease are present at this time.

cultural: Keep up your regular scouting routine so you can catch any early signs of disease or pest activity before they become a problem
Continue pruning to maintain good air movement through the canopy
Follow a balanced fertilization program based on soil test results
organic: Apply preventive sulfur or copper sprays ahead of high-risk weather periods as an insurance measure
Support beneficial insect populations by maintaining diverse ground cover around the orchard

Apple___healthy.prevention: Stay on top of orchard hygiene, scout regularly, and get your preventive treatments in place before the higher-risk seasons arrive.

Blueberry___healthy.description: This blueberry plant looks healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Keep soil pH between 4.5 and 5.5 - blueberries are fussy about acidity, and straying outside this range stresses the plant
Prune out older canes to encourage new, vigorous growth each year
Maintain a layer of mulch around plants to hold moisture and keep weeds down
organic: Apply sulfur sprays preventively during wet seasons when fungal diseases are more likely
Work compost into the soil to support healthy microbiology and improve nutrient availability

Blueberry___healthy.prevention: Keep a regular monitoring schedule, maintain appropriate soil pH, and make sure your beds have good drainage.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on cherries shows up as a distinctive white powder coating on leaves and shoots. It actually thrives in warm, dry weather - contrary to many fungal diseases - as long as humidity is relatively high.
symptoms: A white, powdery fungal coating spreading across the upper surfaces of leaves
Young leaves curling upward and becoming distorted as they try to grow
Infected shoots that are stunted or fail to develop properly
Fruit surfaces developing a russet or patchy white appearance

chemical: Apply myclobutanil, propiconazole, or trifloxystrobin at the first sign of disease - early action makes a big difference
Sulfur-based fungicides are effective, but don't use them when temperatures climb above 30°C or you risk burning the plant
Potassium bicarbonate (Kaligreen) is a good option for knocking back existing colonies quickly
cultural: Open up the canopy through pruning to get better airflow - still air and shade encourage powdery mildew
Go easy on nitrogen fertilizer; lush, soft new growth is exactly what this fungus loves to colonize
Remove and dispose of infected shoots as you spot them throughout the growing season
organic: Weekly applications of potassium bicarbonate or baking soda solution provide ongoing suppression
Neem oil sprays every 7–14 days help keep the fungus in check
Wettable sulfur applied every 7to10 days works well during warm, dry stretches

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Skip overhead irrigation - wet foliage is an open invitation. Keep a close watch during warm, humid spells, and look for resistant varieties if you're replanting.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: This cherry plant is looking healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Prune the tree each year to keep the canopy open and well-structured
Keep an eye out for cherry fruit fly and aphid populations, especially as the season progresses
Make sure the soil drains well - cherries don't tolerate waterlogged roots
organic: Apply copper sprays during dormancy as a preventive measure against fungal and bacterial diseases
Use kaolin clay as a physical barrier to protect against pest insects

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.prevention: Good air circulation, avoiding overwatering, and staying on top of your monitoring routine will go a long way.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot is one of the most yield-damaging fungal diseases that corn growers face, and it can cause serious losses when warm, humid weather persists through the growing season.
symptoms: Rectangular, tan-to-gray lesions with cleanly defined edges that run parallel to the leaf veins - almost like small windowpanes
Lesions that merge together and kill off large sections of leaf tissue
Affected leaves eventually turning brown and papery
In severe cases, the entire plant can die prematurely - a symptom sometimes called 'firing'

chemical: Apply strobilurin fungicides like azoxystrobin or pyraclostrobin around the tasseling to silking stage (VT–R1) for the best economic return
Triazole fungicides such as propiconazole or tebuconazole are effective systemic alternatives
Combining a strobilurin with a triazole in a tank mix gives broad-spectrum protection and is the standard recommended approach
cultural: Planting resistant or moderately resistant hybrids is the single most cost-effective way to manage this disease - start here
Rotating with non-host crops like soybeans or small grains reduces the amount of fungal spores left in the field
Tillage helps bury and break down infected crop residue, cutting off a key source of spores for the following season
Think twice before planting susceptible hybrids in fields with a serious history of gray leaf spot
organic: Crop rotation is the backbone of organic management for this disease
Avoid planting corn in the same field repeatedly, especially where disease pressure has been high
Copper fungicides can offer some partial protection in organic production systems

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.prevention: Scout your fields regularly from the V8 stage through tasseling. If you're seeing disease in the upper canopy before silking, that's your cue to apply a fungicide.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust spreads through wind-carried spores and can travel considerable distances across fields. It tends to be most damaging during cool, humid growing conditions.
symptoms: Small, raised, brick-red to cinnamon-brown pustules scattered across both surfaces of the leaves
Pustules that break open and release powdery, rust-colored spores when touched
Heavy infections where pustules merge together, causing leaves to yellow and die
Pustules turning dark brown or black later in the season as the fungus produces a different spore type

chemical: Apply a strobilurin or triazole fungicide if rust is spotted early and weather conditions are favorable for it spreading
Fungicide applications at VT–R1 give the most economic benefit when disease is already present before tasseling
Both propiconazole and azoxystrobin are proven options
cultural: Choosing resistant or tolerant corn hybrids is your most reliable management tool - make this your starting point
Early planting can help crops get through their most vulnerable stages before rust pressure peaks
Keep a close eye on your fields from V8 onward so you catch any outbreaks early
organic: Selecting resistant hybrids is also the most practical approach in organic production
Sulfur-based sprays may offer limited protection, though results vary
Copper fungicides are registered for rust suppression in certified organic systems

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.prevention: Scout from V8 through R3. A fungicide application is worth considering economically when rust shows up before tasseling under weather conditions that favor its spread.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.severity: Low to Moderate (depends on hybrid susceptibility)

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern leaf blight can cause major yield losses in corn, particularly when it develops before or during the grain-filling stage when the plant most needs its leaves.
symptoms: Long, cigar-shaped lesions (5–15 cm) on leaves, gray-green at first and fading to tan as they age
Lesions with wavy edges that often show a darker olive-green sporulation zone in the middle
Lesions expanding and merging until entire leaves are killed
Disease typically starting in the lower canopy and working its way upward

chemical: Apply fungicides - strobilurins, triazoles, or premixed products - at VT–R1 when you find disease developing below the ear leaf
Premixes combining azoxystrobin and propiconazole give solid broad-spectrum performance
For most situations, a single well-timed application is enough to provide an economic benefit
cultural: Choose hybrids carrying Ht1, Ht2, or Ht3 resistance genes to significantly reduce your vulnerability
Rotate out of corn to break the disease cycle and reduce infected residue levels
Tillage to bury infected crop debris removes a major source of spores for the following year
organic: Crop rotation is the cornerstone of managing this disease organically
Copper fungicides can provide partial suppression when used in certified organic production
Choosing resistant hybrids is especially critical for organic growers without other chemical options

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.prevention: Keep scouting from V8 through grain fill. If you're growing susceptible hybrids and you see early symptoms before tasseling, that's when a fungicide will pay off.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: This corn plant is looking healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Keep soil fertility balanced - use soil tests to guide your nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium applications
Scout fields regularly so you can catch foliar diseases and pest issues before they get out of hand
Make sure plants are spaced adequately so air can flow freely through the canopy
organic: Apply compost to build soil health and keep nutrients cycling naturally
Use cover crops in your rotation to support a healthy, biologically active soil

Corn_(maize)___healthy.prevention: Keep up your regular scouting and maintain good agronomic practices throughout the season.

Grape___Black_rot.description: Grape black rot is one of the most destructive vineyard diseases in humid climates. Under the right conditions, it's capable of wiping out an entire crop in a single season.
symptoms: Circular, tan-to-reddish-brown spots with a dark border appearing on leaves
Tiny black fruiting bodies (pycnidia) visible within leaf lesions
Infected berries turning brown, then shriveling into hard, black, mummified raisins that hang on the vine
Dark, elongated canker-like lesions developing on shoots

chemical: Apply myclobutanil, mancozeb, or captan from bud break through veraison to maintain continuous coverage
Trifloxystrobin (Flint) and azoxystrobin are highly effective and move systemically within the vine
Timing is everything - don't miss applications at bud break, 5–10 cm shoot growth, pre-bloom, bloom, and 2–3 weeks post-bloom
cultural: During dormant pruning, remove and destroy every mummified fruit and infected cane you find - these are next season's infection source
Work on canopy management through shoot positioning, hedging, and targeted leaf removal to improve airflow and reduce humidity
Pull out any infected clusters as soon as you spot them during the growing season
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides such as copper hydroxide or copper sulfate from pre-bloom through cover sprays
Sulfur sprays give good protective coverage but have limited ability to cure existing infections
Neem oil may help suppress early foliar infections

Grape___Black_rot.prevention: The three to four weeks surrounding bloom is the most critical infection window - missing a spray application during this period can be very costly. Plan accordingly and don't skip applications.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).pathogen: Fungal complex (Phaeomoniella chlamydospora, Phaeoacremonium spp., Fomitiporia mediterranea)
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca is a slow-moving but serious trunk disease that gradually destroys grapevine wood from the inside out. In its most severe form, it can cause a vine to collapse suddenly - a dramatic event growers call 'apoplexy'.
symptoms: A striking tiger-stripe pattern of yellowing or reddening between the leaf veins (the color depends on whether the variety is white or red)
Brown to black sunken spots on berry skin that look like scattered measles
Brown streaking and soft, decayed tissue visible when you cut through the wood
Sudden wilting or collapse of entire shoots or the whole vine in late summer - sometimes with no prior warning

chemical: There is no fully effective chemical cure once Esca is established in the vine
Sodium arsenite was historically effective but is now banned in most countries due to toxicity concerns
Trichoderma-based biological products applied to pruning wounds have shown some suppression in trials
cultural: Make clean, precise pruning cuts and apply a wound protectant (paint or paste) right away - the wound is the entry point for the fungi
Try 'double pruning': make preliminary cuts in autumn and your final cuts in late winter to reduce how long wounds stay open to the elements
Remove and burn severely infected vines - leaving them in the vineyard keeps fungal inoculum circulating
Train pruning crews carefully; large, ragged wounds dramatically increase disease risk
organic: Apply Trichoderma harzianum or T. atroviride biological products to fresh pruning wounds as a protective barrier
Garlic extract and essential oil-based wound treatments have shown promising results in research trials
Keep vines in good nutritional balance - vigorous, healthy vines have a better chance of resisting infection

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).prevention: Protect pruning wounds immediately after cutting, and try to avoid pruning during rainy periods when spores are most active.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Grape leaf blight causes dark lesions to develop on older leaves, and if severe enough, the resulting defoliation weakens the vine and drags down fruit quality.
symptoms: Irregular, dark brown to black angular lesions appearing on older leaves
Some lesions surrounded by a yellow halo
A dark olive-gray fuzz of fungal sporulation visible on the undersides of infected leaves
Progressive defoliation of the lower and middle canopy as the season moves toward autumn

chemical: Apply mancozeb, captan, or copper fungicides as soon as you first observe the disease
Strobilurin fungicides offer systemic protection against this pathogen and are a reliable option
Maintain 10–14 day spray intervals when wet conditions persist
cultural: Collect and remove fallen infected leaves from the vineyard floor - they are a key source of spores for the following season
Canopy management through shoot thinning and leaf removal improves air movement and reduces the humidity that encourages the disease
Use drip irrigation rather than overhead systems to keep foliage dry
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides every 7to14 days during the growing season
Sulfur sprays serve as a good protectant during high-risk wet periods
In organic systems, keeping the canopy well-managed is especially important since chemical options are more limited

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.prevention: Keep your canopy open and clean up fallen leaves regularly. Pay special attention to older leaves from mid-season onward, when this disease typically starts showing up.

Grape___healthy.description: This grapevine looks healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Keep up your canopy management work - shoot positioning and leaf removal make a real difference to airflow and fruit quality
Stay alert for powdery mildew, downy mildew, and black rot, especially during wet growing seasons
Apply wound protectants to pruning cuts during dormant pruning to keep trunk diseases out
organic: Apply preventive sulfur or copper sprays ahead of high-risk weather windows
Use Trichoderma-based wound protectants on pruning cuts to protect against trunk diseases

Grape___healthy.prevention: Good vineyard hygiene and consistent monitoring throughout the season are your best ongoing defense.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).pathogen: Bacterial (Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus, spread by Asian citrus psyllid)
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: HLB is one of the most devastating diseases that citrus growers anywhere in the world can face. There is no cure, and infected trees slowly decline and die. The disease is carried and spread almost entirely by the Asian citrus psyllid, a tiny insect.
symptoms: An unusual, patchy yellowing of leaves that doesn't follow the vein pattern - the asymmetry is a telltale sign
Fruit that stays small and misshapen, remaining green on one side even when it should be ripe
Fruit that tastes bitter and has undeveloped seeds pushed to one side of the fruit
Progressive twig dieback and a general decline of the whole tree over time

chemical: There is no cure for HLB - once a tree is infected, it cannot be saved
Thermotherapy (exposing budwood to 48°C heat) has been explored in research settings but isn't practical in the field
Ongoing research is testing trunk injections of oxytetracycline or penicillin, which can temporarily suppress symptoms but do not eliminate the bacteria
cultural: Remove and destroy any confirmed infected trees immediately - every day they remain is a day they're spreading the disease
Aggressively control the Asian citrus psyllid using systemic insecticides like imidacloprid applied as a soil drench
Only purchase nursery trees from certified disease-free sources - never bring untested plant material onto your property
Site new plantings away from known infected groves, and use physical screens in nurseries to exclude the psyllid
organic: Kaolin clay sprays reduce how many psyllids land and feed on the foliage
Neem-based insecticides can help suppress psyllid populations
Reflective mulches deter adult psyllids from settling in the trees
There is no organic cure - all organic management must focus entirely on keeping the psyllid population as low as possible

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).prevention: Preventing the psyllid from spreading the disease is your only real management strategy. Inspect trees monthly and report any suspected cases to your local agricultural authority right away.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot is one of the most economically damaging diseases of peach, causing spotty, blemished fruit, early leaf drop, and cankers on young wood.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that progress to purple-brown lesions with a yellow halo around them
Spots that fall out of the leaf as the tissue dies, leaving a tattered 'shothole' appearance
Dark, sunken, water-soaked spots on fruit that eventually crack open
Elongated, water-soaked cankers on young shoots that turn dark as they develop

chemical: Apply copper-based bactericides starting at shuck split and continue every 7–10 days through harvest
Oxytetracycline (Mycoshield) is effective but needs to be applied before the bacteria enter the plant - timing is critical
Copper plus mancozeb tank mixes improve performance, but avoid applying copper during cool, wet weather when phytotoxicity risk is high
cultural: Planting resistant or tolerant peach varieties is the most effective single management tool available
Avoid sites with poor air drainage - frost pockets and low-lying areas create conditions that favor bacterial spread
Prune to keep the canopy open, and remove infected twigs while the tree is dormant
Switch to drip or micro-spray irrigation to avoid wetting foliage overhead
organic: Apply copper hydroxide or copper sulfate sprays from pre-bloom through harvest
Bacillus subtilis products like Serenade can offer supplemental biological support
Kaolin clay creates a physical barrier that reduces how easily the bacteria can infect the plant

Peach___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Start copper applications at petal fall and don't let gaps develop in your spray coverage - this disease moves fast when the weather turns warm, windy, and wet.

Peach___healthy.description: This peach tree looks healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Apply dormant copper sprays to reduce overwintering fungal and bacterial inoculum before the season starts
Keep the canopy well-pruned for good sunlight penetration and air movement
Monitor for peach leaf curl and bacterial spot throughout the growing season
organic: Apply copper-based sprays during dormancy and at pre-bloom as a preventive measure
Use kaolin clay as a physical barrier against insect pests during the growing season

Peach___healthy.prevention: Regular monitoring and preventive copper applications during dormancy are the foundation of a healthy peach orchard.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot is a serious problem for pepper growers in warm, wet climates. It damages both foliage and fruit, and repeated infections can significantly reduce yield.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that develop brown centers with yellow halos
Lesions merging together and causing widespread defoliation when the disease gets ahead of you
Raised, brown, wart-like or scab-like spots developing on fruit
Dark, water-soaked streaks appearing on stems

chemical: Apply copper hydroxide or copper sulfate mixed with mancozeb every 5–7 days during warm, wet, or windy conditions
Begin your spray program proactively when the weather turns favorable for disease - don't wait for obvious symptoms
Acibenzolar-S-methyl (Actigard) helps the plant build its own resistance - incorporate it alongside your copper program
cultural: Start with certified disease-free or properly treated seed - the infection often enters through the seed
Use clean, sterilized growing media for transplants and never reuse soil from infected seedling trays
Stay out of the field when the foliage is wet to avoid spreading the bacteria on your clothes and tools
Use drip irrigation to keep leaves dry
Rotate peppers with non-solanaceous crops for 2–3 years to reduce soilborne pathogen levels
organic: Apply copper-based bactericides every 5–7 days when weather conditions favor disease development
Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) works as a helpful biological complement to copper programs
Remove and destroy severely infected plants promptly before they become a source of spread to neighboring plants

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Using disease-free seed, avoiding overhead irrigation, and keeping a close eye on your transplants from day one are the most important preventive steps you can take.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: This bell pepper plant is looking healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Make sure plants have enough space between them for good airflow
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry and reduce disease risk
Scout regularly for aphids, thrips, and early signs of disease
organic: Apply compost tea or kelp extract foliar sprays to help strengthen the plant's natural defenses
Use neem oil preventively as a broad-spectrum protection against both fungal and bacterial diseases

Pepper,_bell___healthy.prevention: Good agronomic practices and regular scouting will go a long way toward keeping your pepper crop healthy.

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight is one of the more common fungal diseases potato growers deal with. It targets older leaves first and can strip a significant amount of foliage from susceptible varieties if conditions are right.
symptoms: Dark brown or black circular lesions with concentric rings - resembling a dart board - appearing on the oldest leaves first
Each lesion typically surrounded by a yellow halo
Disease progressing upward from the lower leaves through the canopy over time
Heavy defoliation that reduces the plant's ability to fill tubers to their full potential

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin as soon as you spot the first symptoms
Strobilurin and triazole combination products give you both systemic and protective activity in one spray
Keep up 7–14 day application intervals during stretches of warm, humid weather
cultural: Start with certified disease-free seed tubers - introducing clean planting material eliminates one major inoculum source
Choose resistant varieties when available, especially in fields with a history of early blight
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for at least two years to allow the fungus population in the soil to decline
Keep nitrogen fertility balanced - plants that are underfed are more susceptible to early blight
Hill adequately to reduce soil splash that can carry spores up onto lower leaves
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides every 7–10 days when disease conditions are favorable
Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) can provide a useful level of biological suppression in organic systems
Compost applications help build a more active, suppressive soil biology over time

Potato___Early_blight.prevention: Start your fungicide program once plants reach 15–20 cm in height, or at the very first sign of symptoms on lower leaves - whichever comes first. Also keep nitrogen levels in check.

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is one of the most feared plant diseases in agriculture - this is the same pathogen responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. Under cool, wet conditions, it can wipe out an entire potato crop within just a few days.
symptoms: Water-soaked, pale green lesions on leaves that rapidly turn brown and then black
A white, fuzzy coating of sporulation appearing on the undersides of lesions during humid conditions
Lesions spreading alarmingly fast through the canopy during wet, cool weather
Infected tubers showing reddish-brown, granular rot that extends inward from the skin

chemical: Apply protectant fungicides like chlorothalonil or mancozeb preventively, before disease shows up in the field
Once disease is present, systemic options like metalaxyl-M (mefenoxam), cymoxanil, or dimethomorph are essential
During high-risk periods, spray every 5–7 days without fail - stretching your intervals during an active outbreak is a costly mistake
Rotate between fungicide groups with every application to stay ahead of resistance
cultural: Plant only certified disease-free seed tubers
Choose resistant varieties like Sarpo Mira or Defender - this is the most durable long-term solution available
Remove and destroy any infected plant material immediately; never leave it in the field or pile it on the headlands
Apply a pre-harvest vine desiccant to protect developing tubers in the weeks before you dig
Ensure good drainage and adequate hilling to physically protect the tubers from contact with infected soil
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides such as copper hydroxide or Bordeaux mixture preventively every 5–7 days
Phosphorous acid (Phostrol) is permitted in some organic certification programs and provides some curative activity
Resistant variety selection is especially critical in organic production where chemical options are limited

Potato___Late_blight.prevention: Use a late blight risk forecasting tool like BlightCast to track infection conditions. Start your preventive spray program before the first expected infection period, and act immediately the moment you spot any suspicious symptoms.
Potato___Late_blight.severity: Extremely High (can destroy crop within days)

Potato___healthy.description: This potato plant looks healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Always start with certified seed potatoes - there's no substitute for clean planting material
Keep your fertilization balanced to avoid nutrient stress
Scout fields regularly for early and late blight, especially during cool, wet spells when both diseases can move fast
organic: Apply preventive copper sprays ahead of high-risk weather windows as an insurance measure
Use compost to build soil health and improve overall plant resilience

Potato___healthy.prevention: Certified seed, crop rotation, and consistent weather-based disease monitoring are your best tools for staying ahead of problems.

Raspberry___healthy.description: This raspberry plant looks healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Remove old fruiting canes (floricanes) after harvest - they are a source of cane blight and other diseases for the following year
Thin canes within the row so air can move freely and reduce humidity
Keep a watchful eye for cane blight, spur blight, and botrytis throughout the season
organic: Apply copper sprays during dormancy and in early spring as a preventive measure
Use sulfur sprays during wet periods to suppress fungal disease development

Raspberry___healthy.prevention: Good cane management, annual removal of old wood, and consistent monitoring are the keys to a healthy raspberry planting.

Soybean___healthy.description: This soybean plant is looking healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Rotate soybeans with corn or small grains to interrupt the cycles of key soilborne diseases
Scout regularly for frogeye leaf spot, sudden death syndrome, and soybean cyst nematode (SCN)
Maintain balanced soil fertility to keep plants growing vigorously and reduce stress-related susceptibility
organic: Disease-resistant varieties are the most powerful tool in any organic soybean program
Apply biological seed treatments containing Bacillus subtilis to protect seedlings in the critical early weeks after planting

Soybean___healthy.prevention: Crop rotation and resistant variety selection are the foundation of any effective soybean disease prevention strategy.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew is one of the most common diseases squash growers encounter. Unlike most fungal diseases, it actually prefers warm, dry conditions - though it still needs reasonably high humidity to spread.
symptoms: A white, dusty powder coating spreading across both the upper and lower surfaces of leaves
Infected leaves yellowing, then browning, and dying earlier than they should
Severely affected plants losing most of their foliage
Reduced fruit size and quality in heavily infected plants as they lose their photosynthetic capacity

chemical: Apply trifloxystrobin, myclobutanil, or azoxystrobin at the very first sign of the white coating - catching it early makes all the difference
Potassium bicarbonate products like Kaligreen or MilStop are good options for knocking back existing colonies
Sulfur-based fungicides work well but avoid using them when temperatures are above 32°C to prevent plant damage
cultural: Choose resistant varieties where available - breeders have made great progress on powdery mildew resistance in squash
Avoid overfeeding with nitrogen, which produces lush new growth that the fungus finds especially attractive
Give plants enough room at planting so air flows freely through the canopy
Remove heavily infected leaves and dispose of them away from the garden
organic: Apply potassium bicarbonate or a baking soda solution (1 tablespoon per liter of water) once a week as a preventive and curative spray
Neem oil every 7–14 days provides ongoing suppression
A diluted milk spray (1 part milk to 9 parts water) has shown genuine efficacy in research trials
Wettable sulfur applied every 7 days keeps disease in check during favorable conditions

Squash___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Start checking the upper surfaces of leaves for the first white patches from mid-season onward. This disease spreads quickly once it gets going, so treat at the earliest sign.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Leaf scorch is a common fungal disease in strawberries. When severe, it can noticeably reduce plant vigor and drag down your fruit yields.
symptoms: Numerous small, irregular, dark purple to red-purple spots scattered across the upper surfaces of leaves
Spots merging together into large blotches that give the leaves a scorched, burnt look
Leaves that become brown and dry as the infection takes hold
Noticeably fewer fruit in heavily infected plants

chemical: Apply captan, thiram, or myclobutanil fungicides in early spring and again after renovation in late summer or fall
Strobilurin fungicides like azoxystrobin provide systemic protection when applied every 10–14 days
Post-harvest renovation spray programs are important for reducing the disease load going into the following season
cultural: After harvest renovation mowing, remove old infected leaves from the bed - this is probably the single most impactful thing you can do
Use drip or trickle irrigation to keep foliage dry
Follow a timely renovation program after harvest - mow, thin, and fertilize promptly
Plant resistant varieties where they're available for your region
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides in early spring and again in the fall
Sulfur sprays during wet periods help keep fungal pressure down
Make sure infected leaf debris is cleaned up thoroughly after harvest renovation - it's the main source of spores for next year

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.prevention: Post-harvest renovation and thorough removal of infected foliage are the most important steps you can take. Avoiding overhead irrigation keeps leaves drier and reduces your infection risk.

Strawberry___healthy.description: This strawberry plant is looking healthy with no visible signs of disease.

cultural: Renovate beds after harvest to remove old foliage and reset the disease pressure for the following year
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry and reduce gray mold and leaf disease risk
Scout regularly for gray mold (Botrytis), leaf scorch, and powdery mildew throughout the season
organic: Apply copper sprays preventively in early spring before the season picks up
Lay straw mulch under plants to reduce soil splash and lower fruit rot risk

Strawberry___healthy.prevention: Good bed management, timely renovation after harvest, and consistent scouting are the pillars of keeping strawberry plants healthy year after year.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot is a widespread and commercially important disease of tomato. It damages both foliage and fruit, and tends to flare up during warm, wet, or windy weather.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown and develop yellow margins as they age
Spots merging to create large dead areas, followed by leaf drop in severe cases
Small, raised, dark spots on fruit that develop into rough, scabby lesions
Dark streaks appearing on stems and leaf stalks (petioles)

chemical: Apply copper plus mancozeb tank mixes on a 5–7 day schedule whenever warm, wet, or windy weather is in the forecast
Acibenzolar-S-methyl (Actigard) primes the plant's own immune response - start applications 2–3 weeks before expected disease pressure for best effect
Avoid relying on copper alone at high rates; resistance to copper has been documented in some Xanthomonas strains in certain regions
cultural: Use certified disease-free seed, or treat seed with hot water (50°C for 25 minutes) before planting
Don't transplant seedlings that are already showing symptoms in the tray
Switch to drip irrigation to keep foliage as dry as possible
Rotate away from tomatoes and other solanaceous crops for 2–3 years
Stay out of the field when plants are wet to avoid spreading bacteria from plant to plant on your hands and tools
organic: Apply copper-based bactericides every 5–7 days during disease-favorable weather
Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) provides useful biological suppression as part of an integrated program
Hot water seed treatment (50°C for 25 minutes) is an effective organic approach to reducing seed-borne inoculum

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Start with clean seed and clean transplants. Scout from transplanting onward, and get your copper-based spray program started preventively when weather conditions favor disease.

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight is one of the most frequently encountered fungal diseases in tomato production. It's recognized by its characteristic bullseye-patterned lesions on older leaves, and can also cause rot at the stem end of fruit.
symptoms: Dark brown, circular lesions with concentric rings - like a target pattern - appearing on the lower, older leaves first
A bright yellow halo surrounding each lesion
Canker-like lesions at the soil line (collar rot) in young seedlings
Sunken, dark, leathery spots near the stem end of infected fruit

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin at the first sign of symptoms
Strobilurin plus triazole premix products applied every 7–14 days give both systemic and protective coverage
Boscalid (Pristine) is particularly effective against Alternaria species and is worth considering when pressure is high
cultural: Remove infected lower leaves as soon as you spot them - this slows the upward spread significantly
Stake or cage plants and use drip irrigation to get air moving through the canopy and keep foliage dry
Mulch around the base of plants to prevent infected soil from splashing up onto lower leaves
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for at least two years
Keep nitrogen fertilization moderate - pushing too much growth can actually make plants more susceptible
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides every 7–10 days when conditions favor disease
Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) provides helpful biological suppression in an organic program
Pull off infected lower leaves promptly - this is especially important in organic systems where chemical tools are more limited

Tomato___Early_blight.prevention: Remove infected leaves as soon as they appear. Keep mulch fresh to reduce soil splash. Start your fungicide program at the first sign of symptoms on lower leaves - don't wait for it to climb.

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is one of the most destructive plant diseases in existence - this is the same pathogen that triggered the Irish Potato Famine. Under ideal cool, wet conditions it can completely destroy a tomato crop within just a few days.
symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn pale green at first, then rapidly brown
A white, fuzzy coating of sporulating fungal growth on the underside of lesions in humid conditions
Brown to black, greasy-looking lesions on stems that can girdle and kill entire branches
Fruit developing brown, firm, greasy lesions that rot quickly

chemical: Once disease appears, apply metalaxyl-M (Ridomil Gold) or mefenoxam for systemic, curative control
Protectant fungicides like chlorothalonil, mancozeb, and copper must be in place before infection occurs - they don't work after the fact
Apply every 5–7 days during cool, wet weather and never push your intervals out longer - doing so during an active outbreak can cost you your entire crop
Rotate through different fungicide groups with every application to prevent resistance from developing
cultural: Remove and destroy all infected plant material immediately - bag it in the field before moving it, to avoid releasing spores as you walk
Grow resistant varieties like Defiant, Mountain Merit, or Iron Lady wherever possible
Water in the morning using drip systems, and avoid anything that leaves foliage wet
Never compost infected material - burn it or bury it deeply
Be aware that late blight spores travel many kilometres on the wind, so scout neighbouring properties and community gardens too
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides such as Bordeaux mixture or copper hydroxide preventively on a 5–7 day schedule
Phosphorous acid is permitted in some certified organic programs and provides genuine curative activity - check your certification requirements
Choosing resistant varieties is absolutely essential in organic tomato production

Tomato___Late_blight.prevention: Stay on top of late blight forecasting tools and weather monitoring. Begin preventive copper sprays before the first forecasted infection period. The moment you see any suspicious symptoms, act - delays are extremely costly with this disease.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Tomato leaf mold is primarily a greenhouse and high-tunnel problem, where high humidity creates ideal conditions for it to thrive. Left unchecked, it can cause rapid and extensive defoliation.
symptoms: Pale green to yellow spots appearing on the upper surface of leaves
An olive-green to grayish-purple fuzzy mold growth coating the undersides of those same spots
Infected leaves curling upward, browning, and eventually dropping
Significant defoliation that reduces yield in severely affected crops

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin at the first sign of disease
Trifloxystrobin or myclobutanil provide systemic control that moves within the plant
Spray every 7–10 days for as long as high-humidity disease conditions persist
cultural: Keep greenhouse humidity below 85% by combining active ventilation with supplemental heating when needed
Space plants generously and prune suckers regularly to get good air movement through the canopy
Use drip or subsurface irrigation - avoid any system that wets the foliage
Remove infected leaves and take them outside the greenhouse immediately to prevent spreading spores
organic: Copper-based fungicides applied preventively before humidity climbs provide a useful protective layer
Potassium bicarbonate sprays help reduce disease severity once it's present
Improving ventilation to bring humidity down is the single most impactful organic management step you can take

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.prevention: Keep a close eye on greenhouse humidity. Make sure plants are spaced and ventilated appropriately. Start a fungicide program before humidity gets consistently high, not after the mold appears.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.severity: Moderate to High (especially in protected cultivation)

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot is one of the most common and damaging tomato diseases, often causing severe defoliation that leaves fruit exposed to the sun and reduces both size and quality.
symptoms: Small, circular spots (3–6 mm) with dark brown edges and a grayish-white center appearing on lower leaves first
Tiny black fruiting bodies (pycnidia) visible in the center of spots if you look closely
Disease climbing steadily upward through the canopy over time
Defoliated plants whose exposed fruit suffers from sunscald

chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, copper, or azoxystrobin at the first sign of spots
Spray every 7–10 days during warm, wet weather to maintain coverage
Alternate between fungicide modes of action to reduce the chance of resistance developing
cultural: Remove and destroy infected lower leaves as soon as you notice them
Stake or cage your plants so air can move freely through the canopy
Apply mulch around the base of plants to stop infected soil from splashing onto leaves - this is where most infections start
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 2–3 years
Avoid working in the garden when plants are wet
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides every 7–10 days during the risk period
Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) provides supplemental biological suppression
Heavy mulching to prevent soil splash is particularly important in organic systems where fungicide options are limited

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.prevention: Mulch generously to keep soil from splashing. Remove lower infected leaves at the earliest opportunity. Have your fungicide program ready to go the moment you see the first spots appear.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mites are tiny creatures that pierce individual plant cells and drain their contents, leaving behind stippled, bronzed, and eventually dead tissue. They're most problematic during hot, dry weather and reproduce alarmingly fast when conditions are right.
symptoms: Fine stippling or a bronze tinge to the upper leaf surface where individual mites have been feeding
Delicate webbing visible on leaf undersides and in the spaces between stems
Leaves turning yellow, bronze, or brown before dropping prematurely
Complete defoliation of heavily infested plants in extreme cases

chemical: Apply a dedicated miticide - abamectin (Agri-Mek), bifenazate (Acramite), or spiromesifen (Oberon) are all effective options
Rotate between different miticide classes with every application - spider mites develop resistance remarkably quickly
Horticultural mineral oil or insecticidal soap can knock back all life stages when coverage is thorough
cultural: Be cautious with broad-spectrum insecticides - they often kill the natural predators that would otherwise keep mite populations in check
Keep soil moisture consistent, especially during hot periods - drought-stressed plants are significantly more vulnerable to mite outbreaks
Remove and dispose of heavily infested leaves well away from the growing area
In greenhouse settings, introducing predatory mites from the Phytoseiidae family can provide effective biological control
organic: Neem oil (azadirachtin) sprays every 5–7 days, directed specifically at the leaf undersides where mites congregate
Insecticidal soap (potassium salts of fatty acids) applied directly onto the mites
Release predatory mites such as Phytoseiulus persimilis or Neoseiulus californicus to let nature do the work
Strong jets of water directed at leaf undersides can physically dislodge and disrupt mite colonies

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.prevention: Make it a habit to check leaf undersides regularly, particularly during hot, dry spells. Preserve your natural mite predators by avoiding broad-spectrum pesticides whenever possible. Consistent irrigation also goes a long way.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.severity: Moderate to High (especially under drought stress)

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot has become an increasingly important disease for tomato growers, especially in tropical and subtropical regions and in greenhouse and tunnel production where conditions can favor its spread.
symptoms: Brown lesions with concentric rings - giving them a classic target-like appearance - developing on both leaves and fruit
Yellow halos surrounding lesions on leaves
Dark brown, sunken lesions with concentric rings on fruit
Severe infections causing extensive defoliation and significant fruit losses

chemical: Apply azoxystrobin, difenoconazole, or chlorothalonil as soon as you see the first symptoms
Boscalid plus pyraclostrobin premix (Pristine) provides excellent control and is a top choice when disease pressure is high
Spray every 7–10 days; note that resistance to strobilurin fungicides has been reported in some growing regions, so watch for reduced efficacy
cultural: Stake, prune, and space plants to get good airflow through the canopy - humidity management is key
Use drip irrigation rather than overhead systems
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves promptly so they don't become a source of spreading spores
Rotate crops with non-solanaceous plants to break the disease cycle in the soil
organic: Apply copper-based fungicides preventively every 7–10 days
Bacillus subtilis products offer useful supplemental suppression as part of a broader program
Keeping the canopy open and well-managed is especially critical in organic production

Tomato___Target_Spot.prevention: Start scouting from transplanting onward. In greenhouse production, keep humidity as low as practical. Begin your fungicide program preventively during wet periods - don't wait for obvious symptoms to appear.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.pathogen: Viral (Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus, transmitted by Bemisia tabaci whitefly)
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: TYLCV is one of the most devastating viral diseases a tomato grower can encounter. It's spread by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) and has no cure once a plant is infected - prevention and vector control are everything.
symptoms: Young leaves curling severely upward and cupping, giving infected plants a distinctive crumpled appearance
Yellowing around leaf margins and between the veins
Stunted plants with shortened internodes, giving them a bushy, compact, dwarf-like shape
Flowers dropping without setting any fruit, resulting in severe yield loss or total crop failure

chemical: There is no chemical that cures plants already infected with TYLCV
Control the whitefly vector using imidacloprid soil drenches at transplanting - the systemic action protects the plant from whitefly feeding for weeks
Pyrethroid, spirotetramat, or flupyradifurone foliar sprays help reduce adult whitefly populations
Rotate between insecticide classes to stay ahead of whitefly resistance - this pest develops resistance quickly
cultural: Remove and destroy infected plants the moment you confirm they're infected - every infected plant is a source of virus for nearby healthy plants
Grow TYLCV-resistant or tolerant tomato varieties carrying the Ty-1 or Ty-3 resistance genes wherever possible
Use reflective silver mulch to confuse and deter whiteflies from landing and feeding
Install UV-absorbing greenhouse covers or insect-proof screens to physically exclude whiteflies
Establish crop-free gap periods between plantings to break the whitefly-virus cycle
organic: Neem oil and insecticidal soap help knock back whitefly populations when applied thoroughly and repeatedly
Silver reflective mulch works as a physical deterrent to incoming adult whiteflies
Yellow sticky traps allow you to monitor population levels and can also trap significant numbers when deployed densely
Encarsia formosa parasitoid wasps provide effective biological control in greenhouse settings

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.prevention: Preventing whitefly infestation through resistant varieties and rigorous vector management is the ONLY effective strategy for this disease. If you find infected plants, remove them immediately - every day counts.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.severity: Extremely High (no cure; complete crop failure possible)

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.pathogen: Viral (Tomato Mosaic Virus / Tobacco Mosaic Virus complex)
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus is an exceptionally stable virus that spreads through direct contact with infected plant sap - on contaminated tools, hands, or clothing. It can remain infectious in dried plant material for years, which makes sanitation absolutely critical.
symptoms: A mosaic or patchwork pattern of light and dark green areas across the leaves
Leaf distortion, puckering, or curling that makes infected foliage look uneven
Stunted overall plant growth in more severe infections
Fruit showing internal browning, uneven ripening, or yellow patches

chemical: There is no chemical cure for viral infections in plants
Disinfect all tools between plants using a 10% bleach solution or 70% ethanol - this is not optional
Whole milk applied to hands and tools provides some protection against mechanical transmission of ToMV
cultural: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately upon diagnosis - do not compost them
Use certified virus-free or virus-indexed seed as your starting point
Wash hands thoroughly with soap before handling any tomato plants, and change gloves frequently
Never handle tomato plants after using tobacco products - tobacco can carry TMV and transmit it directly
Disinfect all tools, stakes, and equipment between each use
organic: Organic management is entirely about prevention - there is nothing that cures an infected plant
Milk or skim milk spray applied to hands and tools suppresses the virus and reduces the chance of mechanical spread
Plant resistant varieties carrying the Tm-2 or Tm-2² resistance genes for reliable, long-term protection

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.prevention: Strict sanitation is the foundation of managing ToMV. Use resistant varieties, keep tobacco products well away from your plants, and treat every tool as a potential vector.

Tomato___healthy.description: This tomato plant looks healthy with no visible signs of disease or pest damage.

cultural: Keep up your regular scouting routine so you can catch any early signs of fungal, bacterial, or viral disease before they get established
Maintain consistent irrigation to avoid blossom end rot, which is driven by calcium uptake problems caused by irregular watering
Stake or cage plants to get good airflow through the canopy
Keep mulch fresh around the base of plants to reduce soil splash and help conserve moisture
organic: Apply compost tea foliar sprays periodically to support the plant's natural immune response
Use neem oil preventively every 14 days as a broad-spectrum deterrent
Encourage beneficial insect populations to help keep pest pressure in check naturally

Tomato___healthy.prevention: Regular monitoring and good cultural practices are all that stand between a healthy tomato plant and most of the diseases that commonly affect them. Staying proactive is always easier than responding to an outbreak.
